text: The laparoscopic view shows the sigmoid colon adherent to the vault, initiating the surgery with adhesiolysis using a laparoscopic shearer. The surgeon follows the peritoneal reflection on the lateral pelvic wall to mobilize the sigmoid, exposing the genitofemoral nerve and psoas muscle on the left side. Precise movements of graspers are used to release the sigmoid from the adnexal adhesions, applying blunt traction and counter traction along the fat planes. Energy incisions are made to efficiently mobilize the rectosigmoid from the adnexa, while maintaining the integrity of the adnexal mass.

The surgeon releases the sigmoid colon to access the left pararectal space, ensuring the ureter is safeguarded. The ovarian vessels in the infundibulopelvic ligament are ligated. The surgeon divides the round ligament to open the pelvic triangle, revealing the infundibulopelvic vessels, which are held by the right hand grasper and suture tied. The dissection continues, exposing the left external iliac vein and the obliterated umbilical artery. Ureterolysis is performed, showing the ureter snugly positioned against the lateral aspect of the mass.

The surgeon locates the uterine artery and divides it, facilitating ureterolysis. The obliterated umbilical artery is traced to locate the uterine artery at its origin, which is then sealed and divided. The infundibulopelvic ligament is sealed above the ligated area to create a stump. The fascia below the IP ligament is opened to locate the ureter, which is traced and dissected from surrounding tissues. Attention is shifted to the central vault to lyse bladder adhesions, encountering fibrous adhesions. Counter traction is applied to the mass, and the cleavage plane between the bladder and adnexal mass is developed. The ureter is found intimately involved with the adnexal mass, and the endometriotic cyst is ruptured, releasing chocolate fluid to ease handling. The peritoneum is excised, and the ovary is mobilized.

The surgeon has successfully mobilized the adnexal mass from surrounding tissues, aided by the prior division of the uterine artery. Attention shifts to the pararectal space, where the pararectal fat planes are mobilized, and the inferior part of the mass is extracted. The right adnexa is freed from sigmoid adhesions, following the fat plane to separate the ovary. A rectal probe delineates the rectal axis, and adhesions from the superior aspect of the sigmoid are released, allowing the rectum to stretch on the right side. The rectovaginal space is developed at the central vault, revealing a clear plane for further mobilization. The mass is now largely free except for connections near the bladder and ureter, where severe adhesions and fibrosis are present. The surgeon decides to debulk the mass while keeping some part attached to the ureter to facilitate ureterolysis later.

The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to excise the dense endometriotic fibrous tissue compressing the ureter, while safeguarding the bladder. The ureter is kept in view throughout the procedure. After debulking the tissue, ureterolysis is performed using cold scissors to carefully remove the periureteric endometriotic sheath, moving cranially into a virgin plane to open the tissue around the ureter.

The surgeon excises the remaining endometriotic tissue surrounding the ureter, ensuring the ureter is safeguarded on the left side. The periureteric plane appears supple with preserved vascularity. The posterior planes have been developed, and the tissue is lifted superiorly for complete release. A portion of the involved peritoneum is excised to prevent recurrence. The right adnexa is removed, including the right tube and ovary, with the right infundibulopelvic ligament suture ligated. The final view shows all retroperitoneal spaces opened and tissues appearing supple.
